method2.generated_article:
==Name==
The name Brecqhou refers specifically to the island of Brecqhou, while the name Sark may refer either to the neighbouring island or to the wider jurisdiction that includes Brecqhou and nearby islets and rocks.[1]  
The island is also referred to by the variant spelling Brechou.[5]  
After Jeanne de Chesney married Denis Le Marchant of Guernsey, the island passed through the Le Marchant family and became known as Isle des Marchants.[2]  
The name Isle des Marchants continued to be used even after ownership of the island later passed to others.[2]

==Geography==
Brecqhou, also spelled Brechou, is a smaller, privately owned island in the Channel Islands and is included within the Bailiwick of Guernsey.[2][4] It is geographically associated with Sark, of which it is considered a part, and lies beside Great Sark.[1][4] The island is separated from Great Sark by the narrow Le Gouliot Channel.[4] Brecqhou is part of the Sark–Brecqhou island grouping within the wider Channel Islands, which lie in the English Channel off the coast of Normandy.[1][2] Its separation from Sark by water has contributed to perceptions of Brecqhou as geographically distinct despite its association with Sark.[1] Geologically, Brecqhou forms part of the Bailiwick of Guernsey’s ancient rock assemblage, with metamorphic rocks—principally schists and gneisses—forming most of both Sark and Brecqhou.[5] These rocks record deformation and metamorphism during the early Proterozoic, at about 2,020 million years ago.[5]

==Feudal relationship with Sark==
Sark’s feudal relationship with Brecqhou derives from the 1565 grant by Elizabeth I to Helier de Carteret of the Seignory of Sark, a fief held from the Crown on condition that the island be defended against the monarch’s enemies.[2][3] The grant was associated with a defensive obligation traditionally expressed through forty armed men or forty tenements, and Brecqhou’s proximity to Sark has supported the view that it fell within the same defensive and feudal sphere.[1][3] On this interpretation, Brecqhou forms part of Sark’s fief in the same way that Sark’s authority extends to nearby islets, reefs and rocks.[1]

The status of Brecqhou within the fief has nevertheless been disputed, with Rivett arguing that it was not originally part of the fief and was only later claimed as such.[1] Other accounts have similarly questioned whether Brecqhou was historically a tenement of Sark, while noting that later legal and political practice often treated it as one.[4] A 1949 legal description of the island stated that Brecqhou was a dependency of Sark and that its tenure was similar to the other Sark tenements, although the owner effectively enjoyed a freehold-like holding.[4]

In modern constitutional descriptions, Brecqhou is generally treated as part of Sark, with the Ministry of Justice describing Sark as a jurisdiction within the Bailiwick of Guernsey that includes Brecqhou.[1] Because Brecqhou is treated as a tenement of the fief of Sark, Sark law generally applies there, although some laws have been disapplied to Brecqhou, giving it a special and somewhat autonomous status compared with other tenements.[1] The island’s feudal relationship with Sark became especially contentious after Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay bought Brecqhou in 1993 and challenged aspects of Sark’s seigneurial system, including the treizième payable to the Seigneur on property transfers.[3] Their campaigning and litigation formed part of the wider pressure that preceded the abolition of Sark’s remaining feudal political structure and its replacement in 2008 by an elected assembly.[1][5]

==Fort Brecqhou==
Fort Brecqhou is a granite-clad, castellated Gothic private house on Brecqhou, the small Channel Island next to Sark, built for Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay.[1][2] The Barclay brothers purchased the tenement of Brecqhou in 1993 for £2.3 million, and the estimated cost of the house, gardens and outbuildings was £90 million.[2] Designed by the British architect John Quinlan Terry, the house was begun in 1994 and completed in 1996.[2] It is laid out on a quadrangular plan with circular turrets at the four corners and giant twisted chimneys in a style associated with the reign of Henry VIII.[2] The DiCamillo Companion describes Fort Brecqhou as the largest house built in Britain in the previous 200 years.[2] The building was constructed using two types of Spanish granite, and has been described as resembling a fortress converted into a country house.[1] The house remains a private residence and is not listed, while public access is limited to the grounds by appointment.[2] Peter J. Rivett’s history of the island, *Brecqhou: A Very Private Island*, treats the construction of Fort Brecqhou as part of the island’s modern history under the Barclay brothers.[3]

==Flag and stamps==
Brecqhou has been linked in online flag-related material to a black-and-white flag and to descriptions of it as “the Channel Island that you can't visit”, but the available source does not establish an official flag design, status, or date of adoption.[1] The uncertainty around any such emblem sits within Brecqhou’s wider political context, as scholarship describes the island as privately owned and as having a sometimes contested relationship with Sark, the jurisdiction of which it forms part.[2] Postal material connected with Brecqhou is best understood against the broader Sark and Guernsey context, since Sark is discussed as a British Crown Dependency with micronation-like characteristics, while Guernsey’s postal history includes local stamp issues after postal independence in 1969.[4][5] Guernsey’s postal history developed from informal early mail services to a formally established post office in 1794, use of British stamps from 1840, and later distinctive Guernsey issues, but the available sources do not document a separate official postal administration for Brecqhou.[5]

==Public visits==
Brecqhou is a small, privately owned island lying off the west coast of Sark, within Sark’s jurisdiction in the Bailiwick of Guernsey.[1][5] Since the island was sold to the Barclay brothers in 1993, visits have been by invitation only.[4] As a result, Brecqhou is not generally open for public tourism, unlike nearby Herm, which is described as the smallest of the Channel Islands open to the public.[1][4] The island serves as a residence of the Barclay family and a small number of island employees, while its population is not publicly known.[2]

==Notes and references==
Brecqhou is one of the eight Channel Islands listed in descending order of size after Jersey, Guernsey, Alderney, Sark, Herm, Jethou and Lihou.[1]

The island is about 160 acres in area and lies approximately 85 metres from Sark.[2]

The States of Guernsey has described Brecqhou as part of Sark, so references to Sark as a political jurisdiction may include Brecqhou unless the islands are identified separately.[2]

Brecqhou’s closeness to Sark and its history of sometimes being uninhabited have contributed to ambiguity in references to “Sark”.[2]

Brecqhou has steep cliffs around its coastline, making landing difficult except at the harbour.[2]

Expressions of a separate Brecqhou identity have included Leonard Matchan’s 1960s flag, which adapted the Sark flag by adding his coat of arms, and carriage-label stamp issues beginning in 1969.[2]

Modern political disputes involving Sark have referred to Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay as tenants of Brecqhou and to their influence in debates over Sark’s governance and development.[3]

The Barclay brothers built a large mansion on Brecqhou, visible from boat routes around Sark and nearby islands.[5]

Brecqhou is included on the 2011 States of Guernsey map of the Bailiwick of Guernsey, alongside Guernsey, Sark, Herm, Alderney and Jethou.[4]

Naturalist accounts from Sark describe views toward Brecqhou from the Gouliot headland and boat trips past Brecqhou’s rocky coastline, sheltered coves and small sandy beaches.[5]
